EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pilot reported faint volcanic ash over  St. Croix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains were reported along the central and west part of St.Croix during the late morning and early afternoon. Flooded streets and guts approaching bankfull were reported in in Fredericksted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash floods affected St. Croix Monday night through Tuesday as numerous bands of heavy showers washed out roads and made other impassable. Several residents were rescued from flood waters. Many residential areas were affected by the rains in Fredericksted,  Grove Place, La Grande Princess, Williams Delight, Mon Bijou, Enfield Green, Gallows Bay, Whim, Frangipani and Upper Love.   Roads were washed out and there were some reports of mud and rock slides. Unofficial rainfall amounts varied from 7 to 10 inches overnight into Tuesday.  There were  reports of floating cars being carried away by water currents. Mud washed down the numerous dirt roads in the hills between Fredericksted and Hams Bluff, covering portions of Route 63 with debris.

Police reported that parts of Fangi Pangi, Williams Delight, Melvin Evans Highway, Grove Place and castle Coakley are flooded. Flooding was reported in the area of Lagoon Project. The legislature parking lot and surrounding areas of the Paul E. Joseph Stadium were under water. The Fangi Pangi Gut, the Morning Star bridge, and the La Raine Village were reported flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of November 2003 was the wettest on record for St. Thomas and St. Croix. The ASOS raingage at the St. Thomas airport registered a total of 15.51 inches of rain for the month, breaking the previous record for November of 14.73 inches set in 1984. In St Croix, the ASOS raingage recorded 17.66 inches for the month, breaking the previous record of 16.98 inches set in November 1961.

The month of November 2003 was the wettest on record for St. Thomas and St. Croix. The ASOS raingage at the St. Thomas airport registered a total of 15.51 inches of rain for the month, breaking the previous record for November of 14.73 inches set in 1984. In St Croix, the ASOS raingage recorded 17.66 inches for the month, breaking the previous record of 16.98 inches set in November 1961.

Heavy rains over the U.S. Virgin Islands during the week of November 10-15, 2003, let to widespread flash flooding, mud, land and rock slides. The islands were declared at federal disaster area with damages estimated around 1.5 million. On November 10, 2003, an active late season tropical wave, approached the U.S. Virgin Islands as a mid to upper level trough advanced from the Bahamas towards Eastern Hispaniola. Widespread and intermittent heavy rains affected the U.S. Virgin Islands through early Saturday morning, November 15. Rainfall amount across the U.S. Virgin Island for the period from November 10-15 varied from 10 to 15 inches. The recorded rainfall intensity for St. Croix for the period from November 10-14, exceeded the one hundred year return period.  For St. John approached the one hundred year return period, and for St. Thomas it was below the 25 year return period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains over the U.S. Virgin Islands during the week of November 10-15, 2003, let to widespread flash flooding, mud, land and rock slides. The islands were declared at federal disaster area with damages estimated around 1.5 million. On November 10, 2003, an active late season tropical wave, approached the U.S. Virgin Islands as a mid to upper level trough advanced from the Bahamas towards Eastern Hispaniola. Widespread and intermittent heavy rains affected the U.S. Virgin Islands through early Saturday morning, November 15. Rainfall amount across the U.S. Virgin Island for the period from November 10-15 varied from 10 to 15 inches. The recorded rainfall intensity for St. Croix for the period from November 10-14, exceeded the one hundred year return period.  For St. John approached the one hundred year return period, and for St. Thomas it was below the 25 year return period.

After the period of heavy rains from November 10-15, rainfall tapered off across the U.S. Virgin Islands. However there was a three day stretch of occasional showers associated to a cold front, from November 15-18. Although rainfall amounts were not significant, the ground was so saturated, that even small amounts of rain resulted in additional flooding.  Rainfall amounts varied from 2.6 inches in St. Thomas to 3.65 inches in St. John. This additional rain exacerbated the pre-existent flood situation even more.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pilots and the air traffic control tower reported low visibilities of 4 to 5 miles in volcanic ash.

Heavy rainfall affected St. Thomas and St. John Tuesday and Wednesday. In Estate Pearl and Bonne Esperance rocks and debris carried by water currents covered roads in several places. Guts overflowed everywhere.  Mudslides were reported on the northern end of St Thomas and St John. Mudslides covered parts of Centerline and Northshore road.  All of St. John and the East End of St Thomas lost power since mid afternoon Tuesday. On Wednesday all public schools were closed.
Showers and thunderstorms were producing strong and gusty winds across the higher elevation of St Croix. Spotter at Marienhoj reported southeast winds sustained at 49 mph gusting to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the period of heavy rains from November 10-15, rainfall tapered off across the U.S. Virgin Islands. However there was a three day stretch of occasional showers associated to a cold front, from November 15-18. Although rainfall amounts were not significant, the ground was so saturated, that even small amounts of rain resulted in additional flooding.  Rainfall amounts varied from 2.6 inches in St. Thomas to 3.65 inches in St. John. This additional rain exacerbated the pre-existent flood situation even more.

Lightning struck and killed one person and injured another at the Gordon Finch Molasses Pier on the south shore while loading a boat onto a trailer.  Thunderstoms left several Saint Croix neigborhoods without  power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cessna aircraft flying at four thousand feet reported  volcanic ash cloud six miles southwest of  St. Croix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brush fires fueled by strong winds scorched  hundreds of acres on St Croiox, at Estates Bethlehem, Calguohoun, Cobble, and Lowry Hill. The extremely dry conditions appeared to have caused multiple fires. Several telephone poles were damaged, and some livestock may have perished. About 60 acres were lost in Estate Lowry Hill.

An intense upper to mid level trough west of Puerto Rico  generated showers and thunderstorms that affected the U.S. Virgin Island.  Widespread flooding, rockslides, and road closures were reported.  The rains started on St. Thomas and St. John around 12 noon and continued during the rest of the afternoon and evening. More than three inches of rain were recorded in St Thomas.  Havensight in St. Thomas was one of  the most flood affected areas due to poor drainage. Brookman Road was closed between Four Winds  Plaza and the Bridge to Nowhere. These rains put an end to the drought that had affected the U.S. Virgin Islands since the early part of the year.  Rainfall accumulations of 5.68 inches were recorded at  the St Thomas airport on April 17-18, the St. Croix airport only recorded 2.54 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized urban flooding was reported across St. Croix due  to intermittent heavy rains through the night.

A brush fire was reported near Grassy Point in St Croix. It was burning up in open terrain and hills. A substantial number of acres were burned. Lack of rainfall could have been a contributing  factor.

Low pressure north of the U.S. Virgin Islands  produced heavy rains across the northern Virgin Islands from late morning through mid afternoon.  Minor flooding of low lying areas and small rock slides were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong tropical wave moved across the Island of Montserrat causing the collapse of the volcanic ash dome of Montserrat  Soufriere Hills volcano. Southeast winds carried the volcanic ash towards U.S. Virgin Islands. The ash was deposited the area covering roads, car an vegetation by a thin ash layer. Local aviation operations were impacted by this event.
